Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD's `source` array and top-level content do not pose a risk during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. All sources are either from trusted upstreams (GitHub, Chromium project) or local files. The `source` URLs use HTTPS, and VCS sources pin specific commits, avoiding untrusted mutable references. The `sha256sums` include valid checksums for all sources, even the VCS ones, which is stricter than typical (VCS sources often use SKIP). No command substitutions, `pkgver()` function, or top-level executable code exists that could run during sourcing. The variables used in `source` are defined at the top and static. No `eval`, `base64`, or network exfiltration occurs during parse time. The two commands will safely fetch and verify sources without executing arbitrary code.
</details>
<summary>
All sources verified and pinned; no parse-time execution</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for change_use_of_removed_intrinsic.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a patch for the Skia graphics library, specifically modifying inline assembly code in `Transform_inl.h` to replace a removed LLVM intrinsic (`__builtin_ia32_vcvtph2ps256`) with a modern alternative (`__builtin_convertvector`). This change is part of a legitimate update to maintain compatibility with newer versions of LLVM.

The patch originates from a Google developer and is associated with tracked bugs and code reviews on the official Skia project (skia-review.googlesource.com). The change is narrowly focused on low-level vector type conversion using well-defined compiler intrinsics, which is normal in high-performance graphics code. There are no network calls, file operations, obfuscated commands, or system interactions beyond standard compile-time intrinsics.

All modifications are confined to a single source file and align with expected upstream development practices. The use of `__builtin_convertvector` is a safe, documented GCC/Clang feature. No malicious behavior, backdoors, or supply-chain risks are present.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate code update for compiler compatibility</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed change_use_of_removed_intrinsic.patch. Status: SAFE -- Legitimate code update for compiler compatibility
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches Aseprite and its required components (Skia, ICU, and translations) from official and trusted upstream sources: GitHub repositories for Aseprite and its affiliated projects, and a Chromium Google source repository for ICU. All source URLs are verifiable and correspond directly to the project's dependencies. Checksums for all included files (including patches) are provided and validated via `sha256sums`, ensuring integrity. The build process applies a series of patches to enable shared library usage and improve integration with the system, which is common in AUR packages to reduce bloat and improve maintainability. These patches are included in source control and their contents are transparent.

No network operations occur during the build or prepare phases beyond the initial source downloads, which are static and pinned to specific commits or versions. The package compiles Skia and Aseprite from source with clang, as recommended upstream, and installs only the necessary binaries, icons, desktop files, MIME data, and documentation into the package directory. The use of `bsdtar` for selective extraction and careful `install`/`cp` operations to control what goes into the final package is a legitimate packaging practice. There is no obfuscated code, no runtime downloads, no data exfiltration, and no unauthorized modifications to system directories outside of `$pkgdir`. The inclusion of debug prints controlled by `_debug` is benign.

All operations align with standard AUR packaging practices. While the Skia build pulls ICU from an external but appropriate source (Chromium's ICU), it does so from a pinned commit, eliminating risk of unexpected code fetches. The package does not run `git pull` or similar mutable operations during build. Dependencies are clearly listed and correspond to Aseprite's legitimate requirements. No dangerous commands (e.g. curl|bash, eval, base64-decoded payloads) are used.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR package with transparent, verifiable sources and safe build process
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for shared-libwebp-found.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a patch for the CMakeLists.txt file in the aseprite package. It modifies the build configuration to explicitly set `WEBP_FOUND ON` when the required WebP libraries and headers are located, which ensures the build system recognizes that libwebp is available. This change simplifies the detection logic by directly setting the `WEBP_FOUND` flag instead of relying solely on the outcome of subsequent conditions.

There are no signs of malicious behavior in this patch. It does not introduce network requests, obfuscated code, or unexpected system modifications. The modification is consistent with standard packaging practices aimed at improving compatibility or reliability in detecting dependencies. The change only affects the local build configuration and has no runtime side effects or security implications.

The patch is focused solely on build logic and does not alter program behavior beyond enabling correct linking against the system's libwebp. It does not introduce any backdoors, telemetry, or unauthorized data access.
</details>
<summary>
Benign build configuration change for libwebp detection</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a patch for a C++ header in the Skia graphics library, part of the Aseprite package. It adds an explicit include of <cstdint>, which provides standard integer types like uint32_t. This is a common and legitimate change to ensure proper type definitions are available, especially in cross-platform code.

There are no signs of malicious activity: no network operations, no obfuscated code, no unexpected file or system modifications. The change is minimal, focused, and consistent with normal software maintenance. The use of standard C++ headers is benign and expected in native codebases.

Patch files in AUR packages often contain such small fixes to improve compatibility or fix build issues. This one shows no evidence of supply chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Benign patch adding standard C++ header include</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for desktop.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `desktop.patch` file contains two simple modifications to desktop integration files for the Aseprite application. The first change updates the `Exec` line in the `.desktop` file from `%U` (URLs) to `%F` (files), which is a legitimate adjustment to ensure the application correctly handles file paths when launched from a file manager. The second change updates the MIME type icon from `aseprite` to `image-x-aseprite`, aligning the icon name with standard naming conventions for MIME types. Both changes are benign and relate only to desktop environment integration and user experience.

There are no executable commands, network operations, obfuscated code, or file system modifications beyond the scope of the patch. The changes are transparent, human-readable, and consistent with standard packaging and desktop integration practices for Linux applications. No malicious behavior such as data exfiltration, remote code execution, or unauthorized system access is present or implied.

This patch only affects how the application is launched and how its file type is represented in the desktop environment—no supply-chain risks are introduced.
</details>
<summary>
Benign desktop integration changes, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/10] Reviewing src/CMakeLists.txt, fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch, optional-pixman.patch, shared-libarchive.patch, shared-fmt.patch, shared-libwebp.patch, shared-skia-deps.patch...
+ Reviewed desktop.patch. Status: SAFE -- Benign desktop integration changes, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for CMakeLists.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `CMakeLists.txt` file is part of the Aseprite build configuration and contains standard CMake directives used to configure compilation options, manage dependencies, and set up build paths. It checks for the presence of required submodules and provides helpful error messages if they are missing, directing users to use `git submodule update --init --recursive` or download complete source archives. All declared dependencies (e.g., zlib, libpng, curl, freetype) are either bundled in the `third_party` directory or optionally linked via system libraries based on user-controlled CMake options.

Several features such as `ENABLE_UPDATER`, `ENABLE_NEWS`, `ENABLE_SENTRY`, and `ENABLE_WEBP` are guarded by options and do not exhibit malicious behavior. While `ENABLE_SENTRY` allows crash reporting with a configurable DSN, the DSN is empty by default and must be explicitly set by a developer or distributor. This represents a privacy consideration only if activated and configured, but not a supply-chain attack. The file does not perform any network operations, downloads, or code execution outside of the normal build process. It also does not contain obfuscated code, unexpected file manipulations, or data exfiltration mechanisms.

The use of bundled third-party libraries is typical for C/C++ projects to ensure reproducible builds and avoid dependency drift. Options like `USE_SHARED_*` allow opting into system libraries if desired. No remote code execution, unauthorized telemetry activation, or insecure default behaviors are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard build configuration; no malicious code detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[4/10] Reviewing fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch, optional-pixman.patch, shared-libarchive.patch, shared-fmt.patch, shared-libwebp.patch, shared-skia-deps.patch...
+ Reviewed src/CMakeLists.txt. Status: SAFE -- Standard build configuration; no malicious code detected.
LLM auditresponse for shared-skia-deps.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a patch (`shared-skia-deps.patch`) modifying the CMake configuration for Skia in the Aseprite build system. It conditionally disables the use of bundled FreeType and HarfBuzz libraries when the `USE_SHARED_FREETYPE` or `USE_SHARED_HARFBUZZ` flags are set, allowing the build to use system-installed versions instead. This is a common and legitimate packaging practice to reduce duplication and improve maintainability by leveraging shared system libraries.

The patch modifies `FindSkia.cmake` to wrap `find_library` calls for FreeType and HarfBuzz in `if(NOT USE_SHARED_*)` blocks, ensuring these internal copies are only used when shared libraries are not requested. There is no executable code, no network operations, no file manipulation outside the build context, and no obfuscated or suspicious commands. The changes are purely declarative and consistent with standard CMake logic for dependency resolution.

This patch supports flexible, secure, and reproducible builds by enabling integration with system libraries. It does not introduce any malicious behavior, backdoors, or supply-chain risks.
</details>
<summary>
Patch enables shared library use; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[5/10] Reviewing fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch, optional-pixman.patch, shared-libarchive.patch, shared-fmt.patch, shared-libwebp.patch...
+ Reviewed shared-skia-deps.patch. Status: SAFE -- Patch enables shared library use; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for shared-libarchive.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided patch file `shared-libarchive.patch` modifies CMake build configuration to add an option `USE_SHARED_LIBARCHIVE` that allows using a system-installed version of libarchive instead of the bundled static version. The changes are limited to CMake logic: adding the option, conditionally finding the shared library via `find_package(LibArchive REQUIRED)`, and wrapping the local `libarchive` subdirectory inclusion in a condition to avoid conflicts when using the shared version. The patch also updates the linking directive in `src/app/CMakeLists.txt` to use `${LibArchive_LIBRARIES}` instead of the hardcoded `archive_static`.

There is no evidence of malicious behavior. The modifications are consistent with standard packaging practices aimed at reducing binary bloat and improving maintainability by leveraging system libraries. No network operations, file manipulations outside the build context, obfuscated code, or dangerous commands are present. The changes affect only build-time configuration and do not introduce backdoors, telemetry, or data exfiltration mechanisms. This is a typical enhancement found in Linux distribution packaging to support shared libraries.

The author and commit metadata appear legitimate, and the change aligns with common practices in downstream patches for software like Aseprite that bundle third-party libraries. Using shared libraries can improve security through centralized updates and auditing, assuming the shared library is properly maintained by the system.
</details>
<summary>
Build configuration change to use shared libarchive; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[6/10] Reviewing fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch, optional-pixman.patch, shared-fmt.patch, shared-libwebp.patch...
+ Reviewed shared-libarchive.patch. Status: SAFE -- Build configuration change to use shared libarchive; no malicious content

<details>
The provided file is a patch to a CMakeLists.txt file, modifying the build configuration for the Aseprite project. It adds a conditional check to skip pixman-related configuration when the LAF_BACKEND is set to "skia". This change is consistent with typical build system adjustments and does not introduce any malicious behavior.

There are no network operations, file exfiltration attempts, obfuscated commands, or unexpected system modifications. The patch exclusively alters the build logic by conditionally including pixman dependencies, which is a standard practice in CMake-based projects. The use of `STREQUAL` and conditional blocks is normal CMake syntax and does not pose a security risk.

The change is limited to build-time logic and does not affect runtime behavior in a way that would compromise system security. It aligns with expected packaging practices and upstream build customization. No checksums, downloads, or executions are involved in this patch.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for shared-libwebp.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a patch for CMakeLists.txt files in the Aseprite source code, enabling optional use of a shared system WebP library via the `-DUSE_SHARED_WEBP` CMake option. The changes are consistent with standard CMake packaging practices and introduce no malicious behavior.

It adds a new CMake option to link against system-installed WebP libraries instead of bundling them. The patch modifies build logic to conditionally use `find_library` and `find_path` to locate system WebP components when requested. These are standard CMake functions for dependency resolution and do not introduce security risks.

The patch does not include network operations, code execution, file exfiltration, or obfuscated commands. It solely alters the build configuration to support system library dependencies, a common and acceptable practice in Linux packaging. There is no evidence of supply chain tampering or injected malicious code.
</details>
<summary>
Patch adds shared WebP support safely
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[8/10] Reviewing fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch, shared-fmt.patch...
+ Reviewed shared-libwebp.patch. Status: SAFE -- Patch adds shared WebP support safely
LLM auditresponse for fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch` is a patch for a CMakeLists.txt file that modifies the build configuration of the TinyEXIF project. It adds `find_package(tinyxml2 REQUIRED)` to locate the tinyxml2 library at build time and appends an `export(TARGETS ...)` command to support CMake's `find_package` config mode. These changes are standard practices in CMake-based projects and do not introduce any malicious behavior.

There are no network operations, file exfiltrations, or suspicious commands present. The patch modifies only the build system to properly link against an external dependency (tinyxml2) and ensure proper target export, which aids in downstream integration. All changes are transparent, readable, and consistent with legitimate packaging improvements.

No obfuscation, unauthorized downloads, or system modifications are included. The patch aligns with common CMake project configurations and poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Patch adds legitimate CMake dependency and export targets
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[9/10] Reviewing shared-fmt.patch...
+ Reviewed fix-shared-tinyxml2.patch. Status: SAFE -- Patch adds legitimate CMake dependency and export targets
LLM auditresponse for shared-fmt.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided patch file `shared-fmt.patch` modifies the CMakeLists.txt files in the aseprite project to support using a system-installed version of the `fmt` library instead of the bundled one. The changes are consistent with standard software packaging practices, particularly in the context of AUR packages where users often prefer shared libraries to reduce duplication and improve maintainability.

The patch introduces a new CMake option `USE_SHARED_FMT`, which when enabled, uses `find_package(FMT REQUIRED)` to locate the system-installed `fmt` library. If disabled (the default), it falls back to the bundled `fmt` in the `third_party/fmt` directory. This is a common and safe pattern in CMake-based projects. The modifications to `target_link_libraries` to use `${FMT_LIBRARIES}` instead of a hardcoded `fmt` are also standard and safe.

There are no network operations, no code downloads, no obfuscation, and no suspicious file or system manipulations. The changes are limited to build configuration and do not introduce any runtime behavior that could compromise security. The use of `fmt::fmt` as a target when using the shared library is consistent with the official `fmt` library's CMake integration.
</details>
<summary>
Adds fmt library sharing option safely
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[10/10] Reviewing ...

The `source` of `platform.sh` is a normal pattern for sharing configuration. The script only outputs the URL; it does not download or execute anything. LLM auditresponse for desktop.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The patch makes two small changes to Aseprite's Linux desktop integration files. First, it changes the `Exec` field in the `.desktop` file from `%U` (URLs) to `%F` (files), which is a common correction for an application that opens local files rather than remote URLs. Second, it updates the MIME type icon name in `aseprite.xml` from `aseprite` to `image-x-aseprite`, aligning with freedesktop.org naming conventions. Neither change involves network requests, obfuscation, dangerous system modifications, or any behavior that deviates from standard packaging practices. The modifications are entirely benign and improve proper desktop integration.
</details>
<summary>Standard desktop integration patch, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

This patch introduces a `USE_SHARED_FMT` build option (mimicking existing shared library options like `USE_SHARED_CMARK`, `USE_SHARED_CURL`) to allow using the system-installed `fmt` library instead of the bundled `third_party/fmt` version. There is no obfuscation, no network requests, no unexpected commands, and no tampering with files outside the build system. The changes are limited to CMake logic that is entirely standard for AUR packaging workflows. No security concerns.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for build.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `build.sh` is a build helper script for the Aseprite project. It automates the process of configuring, downloading dependencies (Skia), and building the application via CMake and Ninja. All operations are standard for a C++ project build system: checking dependencies (`cmake`, `ninja`, `cl.exe`), managing git submodules, running `cmake` and `cmake --build`, and optionally downloading a pre-compiled Skia library from the project’s own upstream repository. The script does not contain obfuscated code, base64-decode, eval, or any unexpected network requests to unknown hosts. It does not exfiltrate data, modify system files outside the build/checkout directories, or install backdoors. The download of Skia is performed via `curl` from a URL generated by a script within the project’s own repository (`laf/misc/skia-url.sh`), which is legitimate upstream behavior. The “Update SDK” feature that modifies cmake cache files is part of normal Windows development workflow and not malicious. While downloading pre-compiled binaries carries inherent supply-chain risk, that risk originates from the upstream project, not from this helper script itself. The script makes no attempt to deviate from the intended packaging or build workflow.
</details>
<summary>Standard build helper; no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>
